item.title: Is president preparing to leave Bangabhaban?
item.description:
Preparations are underway for President Mohammed Shahabuddin to move to his private residence in Gulshan, as multiple sources say he is expected to resign from office, citing health reasons, within the next one or two days.
According to several sources, Shahabuddin may submit his resignation letter to the speaker of parliament as early as Friday afternoon or within the next two days, in line with the Constitution.
Sources said arrangements for his relocation from Bangabhaban to his Gulshan residence have already begun.
Officials familiar with the matter said preparations related to the resignation at the President's Office are in their final stages and that the resignation letter has already been drafted.
Under the Constitution, the president must submit a written resignation to the speaker of parliament for it to take effect.
Meanwhile, Speaker Major (Retd) Hafiz Uddin Ahmed, who had been in Thailand for medical treatment, returned to Bangladesh on Friday ahead of schedule. Although the government has not officially explained why the visit was cut short, officials believe the decision may be linked to the anticipated resignation process.
Several Bangabhaban officials said Shahabuddin has, in principle, decided to step down. They also claimed that some officials appointed during his tenure have already started packing their personal belongings.
Speaking on condition of anonymity, one Bangabhaban official said the president verbally instructed subordinate officials a few days ago to vacate the presidential residence by the end of this month.
The official added that after moving to his Gulshan residence, Shahabuddin plans to travel abroad for medical treatment and rest.
Neither the government nor the President's Office has officially commented on the reported resignation or the planned relocation.
Under Article 54 of the Constitution, if the office of the president becomes vacant or the president is unable to perform his duties, the speaker of parliament will serve as acting president until a new president is elected.
Under Article 123(1), a new president must be elected within 90 days of the office becoming vacant.
Shahabuddin assumed office as Bangladesh's 22nd president in April 2023. During his tenure, he has on several occasions publicly indicated his willingness to step down before completing his term, most recently saying he would remain in office if the government wanted him to, but would resign voluntarily otherwise.
No official announcement has yet been made regarding his reported resignation or his move to the Gulshan residence.

item.title: Speaker outlines constitutional process if president resigns
item.description:
National Parliament Speaker Hafiz Uddin Ahmad on Friday said that if the president resigns, the resignation must be submitted to the speaker, who will then assume the role of acting president.
Speaking to reporters at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport after returning from Thailand, the speaker said that under the Constitution, he would perform the duties of acting president if the office becomes vacant.
He added that a new president must be elected by parliament within 90 days, in accordance with the Constitution.
Responding to a question, Hafiz Uddin said he returned to Bangladesh because his scheduled medical check-up in Thailand had been completed.
Earlier in the day, the speaker arrived at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport at 12:10pm on Biman Bangladesh Airlines flight BG321.
President Mohammed Shahabuddin is expected to resign from office, citing health concerns, according to multiple sources familiar with the matter.
Several sources said the BNP government has agreed in principle to the move following discussions with the president. They added that Shahabuddin intends to travel to a Southeast Asian island nation after stepping down.
President's Press Secretary Mohammad Sarwar Alam confirmed to a national media outlet that Shahabuddin had expressed his intention to resign and that the government had agreed to the proposal.

item.title: Speaker returns home after Thailand visit
item.description:
Speaker of the National Parliament Hafiz Uddin Ahmad, Bir Bikram, MP, returned home today after a visit to Thailand.
The speaker arrived at Hazrat Shahjalal International Airport in Dhaka at around 12:15pm after concluding his visit to Bangkok.
Earlier, on July 19, Hafiz left Dhaka for the Thai capital on a short visit, according to an official statement issued by the National Parliament Secretariat.

item.title: Is President Sahabuddin leaving office?
Measles outbreak: Death toll reaches 810 as five die in 24 hours
item.description:
Five children died with measles-like symptoms in the 24 hours until 8:00am on Friday, bringing the combined tally of confirmed and suspected measles-related deaths to 810 in the country.
According to the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS), all the latest fatalities were suspected measles deaths.
With the latest update, the number of suspected measles deaths has risen to 715, while the number of laboratory-confirmed measles deaths remained 95.
A total of 950 new suspected measles cases were reported during the 24-hour period, bringing the cumulative number of suspected cases nationwide to 122,150.
Meanwhile, 187 new confirmed measles cases were recorded, bringing the total number of laboratory-confirmed infections to 15,194.
Since March 15, a total of 104,717 patients with suspected measles have been hospitalized across the country, of whom 101,056 have recovered, according to DGHS data.

item.title: US slaps 10% tariff on Bangladesh imports citing forced labor concerns
item.description:
The United States has imposed new additional tariffs on imports from 60 trading partners, including Bangladesh, accusing them of failing to take sufficient measures to prevent the import of goods produced through forced labor.
The new tariffs took effect on Friday, with Bangladeshi exports facing an additional 10% duty. The move is expected to put pressure on Bangladesh's export-oriented industries, particularly the ready-made garment (RMG) sector.
According to Reuters, the measure is part of President Donald Trump's broader effort to reshape the global trading system. The White House said the tariffs target countries that have not adopted enforcement measures against forced labor comparable to those of the United States.
A notice published in the Federal Register said the new tariffs will apply to approximately 99.4% of all imports into the United States. However, oil and gas, fertilizers, certain food products, and several national security-related goods will be exempt.
Under the final list, Bangladesh joins Argentina, the United Kingdom, Cambodia, Canada, Ecuador, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, India, Indonesia, Jordan, Malaysia, Mexico, Pakistan, Sri Lanka, and Trinidad and Tobago in facing an additional 10% tariff on exports to the US.
Meanwhile, for the European Union, Taiwan, Japan, South Korea, and Switzerland, the total tariff rate has been set at 10% or 12.5%, depending on existing Most-Favored-Nation (MFN) duties.
An additional 38 countries, including China, will face a 12.5% tariff. The US has accused China of using forced labor involving the Uyghur minority, an allegation Beijing has consistently denied.
The tariffs have been imposed under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974, allowing the US to maintain minimum import duties on most goods despite a recent US Supreme Court ruling.
Analysts say legal challenges to the new tariffs may face difficulties because Section 301 has previously withstood judicial scrutiny.
US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer said: "For nearly a century, the United States has maintained a strong stance against imports made with forced labor. It is time for our trading partners to adopt similarly effective measures."
Trump administration officials said the tariffs are intended not only to reduce trade imbalances but also to address human rights violations and prevent unfair trade advantages.
They argued that the United States has the world's strictest import controls against products made with forced labor, while many other countries continue to benefit from weaker enforcement.
The new tariffs will not apply to oil and gas, fertilizers, certain food products, or goods already covered by separate national security tariffs, including automobiles, steel, aluminum, and copper.
Products that qualify under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement (USMCA) are also exempt, reflecting the integrated North American supply chain and the high level of US content in those goods.
The United States is one of Bangladesh's largest export markets, with ready-made garments accounting for the bulk of the country's exports.
The additional 10% tariff could weaken Bangladesh's export competitiveness, particularly in the apparel sector, by increasing costs for US buyers.
The decision also underscores the growing importance of complying with international standards on labor rights, supply chain transparency, and the prevention of forced labor.

item.title: Bangladesh received ‘lowest’ tariff rate under new US decision
item.description:
Bangladesh has received the “lowest” tariff rate under a new US decision imposing levies on imports from 60 trading partners over the use of forced labour, the Ministry of Foreign Affairs said on Friday.
While Bangladesh faces the 10% tariff, several major global apparel manufacturing competitors, specifically China, Vietnam and Thailand, will face the maximum 12.5% tariff rate.
The government of Bangladesh said it remains fully committed to upholding international labour standards and will continue to engage closely with international partners to ensure the robust growth, compliance, and sustainability of Bangladesh's critical export sectors.
The US government on Thursday announced the imposition of tariffs under Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 on 60 economies representing 86 countries globally.
This measure comes after investigations conducted by the Office of the United States Trade Representative (USTR) regarding the use of forced labour.
Effective Friday, the tariff ranges from a low of 10% to a high of 12.5%.
This replaces the temporary 10% global tariff previously implemented under Section 122.
For Bangladesh, the tariff will be 10% on all imports originating from the country.
It will be applied in addition to existing Most-Favoured-Nation (MFN) tariff rates. Bangladesh is among the 17 out of 86 countries globally that have been placed under the lower 10% tariff tier.
Therefore, it retains Bangladesh's competitive standing in the US apparel and export market, said the ministry.
This distinct differential reinforces Bangladesh's ongoing comparative advantage in the US market relative to its high-tariff competitors, it said.
Additionally, the USTR is considering establishing a three-year Tariff-Rate Quota (TRQ) for four countries, including Bangladesh, Cambodia, Indonesia and Malaysia, to waive Section 301 tariffs on goods made from US cotton and textile inputs.
This, when implemented, will also provide Bangladesh further competitive advantage and promote Bangladesh’s exports to the US market.

item.title: Fakhrul urges people to ignore ‘rumours’ over President's resignation
item.description:
BNP Secretary General Mirza Fakhrul Islam Alamgir on Friday urged people not to pay heed to “rumours” surrounding the possible resignation of President Mohammed Shahabuddin.
While speaking to journalists after attending the inaugural session of the two-day Bengal Delta Conference 2026 at Hotel InterContinental Dhaka, he dismissed the speculation and called on the public not to be misled by unverified information.
His comments came amid widespread speculation that the President may step down in a day or two, citing ill health.
"The entire world is facing challenges to democracy in one way or another. But we have always believed in the democratic process," Fakhrul, also local government, rural development and co-operatives minister, said.
He said the current government remains accountable to the people and is committed to upholding democratic values and public accountability.

item.title: Four Palestinians killed by Israeli forces in West Bank
item.description:
Four Palestinians were killed and four others wounded on Friday after Israeli occupation forces attacked the village of Tell, southwest of Nablus, according to the Palestinian Ministry of Health.
The ministry said three of the injured were in critical condition after being shot during confrontations that erupted following the attack, reports WAFA News Agency.
Issam al-Saifi, secretary of the Fatah movement in Tell, said Israel first attacked two homes in the eastern part of the village, prompting residents to intervene and drive them away.
He said the forces returned about 30 minutes later and launched another attack in the western part of the village. Clashes then broke out between local residents and the colonists and Israeli occupation forces, who entered the area, he alleged, to protect the colonists.
According to al-Saifi, Israeli troops and colonists opened fire during the confrontation, killing four Palestinians—two brothers and two of their cousins—and injuring four others. He added that Israeli forces later declared the village a closed military zone while settlers remained in the surrounding area.
The Palestinian Ministry of Health said 82 Palestinians have been killed across the occupied West Bank since the start of the year, including 21 who it said were killed by Israeli settlers.

item.title: PM's adviser: Jobs key to Bangladesh's next growth phase
item.description:
Outlining the government's economic strategy, Prime Minister's Finance and Planning Adviser Dr Rashed Al Mahmud Titumir on Friday said Bangladesh needs an investment-driven development model tailored to domestic realities and changing global geoeconomic conditions.
"We need to pursue a model where investment leads to production, production creates employment, employment increases revenue without raising tax rates, and that revenue finances education, health and social security," he said.
The economist made the remarks during a session at the Bengal Delta Conference 2026 (BDC 2026) moderated by Mushtaq Khan, a Professor of Economics at SOAS University of London.
The Bengal Delta Conference 2026 (BDC 2026), themed “Bangladesh and a Changing World: Uncharted Times, Emerging Orders, and The Politics of Care,” comes at a moment of transition for both Bangladesh and the global order
Dr Titumir reiterated the government's goal of gradually increasing spending on education, healthcare and social protection while moving towards a democratic welfare state.
The Adviser said the government is working to introduce a universal lifecycle-based social security system to protect vulnerable people from falling into poverty during economic shocks.
He also stressed reforms in education and healthcare, saying the focus would be on skills development, citizenship education, innovation and building a national health system reaching grassroots communities.
Highlighting governance reforms, Dr Titumir said legitimacy, accountability and transparency are central to the government's approach.
"If you have a government with legitimacy, you have accountability," he said, adding, "The delivery is about legitimacy. The delivery is accountability. The delivery is transparency."
He said the government inherited a fragile fiscal situation but now has both the political mandate and responsibility to pursue transformative reforms.
"We all are aware of what we inherited. The fiscal condition we inherited was fragile," he said. "There is an overwhelming majority in Parliament, which means that we have more responsibility, and we have to have a transformative change."
Responding to questions on revenue mobilisation, Dr Titumir said the government is prioritising institutional reforms over higher tax rates.
"The equation that the current government is working on has two additional elements which were ignored for years - the constant and the error term," he said, explaining that the "constant" represents institutional strength while the "error term" includes corruption, rent-seeking and tax exemptions.
He said reducing leakages and improving enforcement had already produced encouraging results.
"For the first time in the history of Bangladesh, Chattogram Customs House made a huge contribution," he said, adding that three task forces had been formed to strengthen revenue collection.
Acknowledging existing challenges, Dr Titumir said the government is not claiming to have solved every problem.
"We are not fully equipped yet to deal with tax evasion. That requires capabilities," he said.
On the financial sector, he said authorities are addressing long-standing issues, including non-performing loans and bank recapitalisation.
"We are not hiding anything under the carpet. We are aware of the issues, and we are dealing with them," he said.
Dr Titumir said the government is committed to ending what he described as the "auction market" for Statutory Regulatory Orders (SROs), strengthening accountability and pursuing a production-led economic model to achieve sustainable growth.
"We have inherited what is known as an auction market for SROs. If you are powerful, you get an issuance of an SRO, and you get all those resources," Titumir said while outlining the government's reform agenda.
Referring to tax concessions granted in the past, he said, "These arbitrary concessions, we are working on them, and we are making sure that this SRO culture is not there."
The adviser said decisions on taxation and public expenditure must be guided by Parliament rather than discretionary executive actions.
"It is the sovereign Parliament that would decide where and how the money would be collected and where the money would be spent. This is the key essence of the reform of the government," he said.
Calling industrialisation critical for long-term resilience, Dr Titumir said Bangladesh has revived discussions on industrial policy after years of neglect.
"Industrial policy is back in Bangladesh," he said, adding that expanding productive capacity, ensuring energy security, improving competitiveness and diversifying exports would be essential as the country prepares for graduation from the Least Developed Country (LDC) category in 2029.
Expressing optimism about the government's reform agenda, Titumir said the administration expects to deliver tangible results during the current fiscal year.
"This fiscal year, you would get the early harvest," he said. "We are hopeful that Bangladesh, what we have promised, we would deliver."
Referring to external economic pressures, he said Bangladesh had to spend about $3.46 billion to absorb the impact of the Middle East crisis and global energy price shocks despite having no role in creating those challenges.
"We had to spend $3.46 billion for nothing to do with us," he said, noting that the government had to balance fiscal management with protecting agriculture and consumers from higher fuel costs.

item.title: The need for security
item.description:
Any nation looking to take the next step in its development journey cannot do so without having the defense architecture that keeps pace with the growth.
To that end, we must heed the warning from security and technology experts who have made it clear that Bangladesh’s critical infrastructure is increasingly vulnerable.
In particular, given the exponential expansion of technology and how it is being weaponized for nefarious purposes, beyond simply physical defense, we must urgently strengthen our cyber spaces as well.
Let it be known that this is not a theoretical concern but a real, immediate national security issue, one that Bangladesh can no longer afford to treat as secondary.
Essential systems such as power grids, telecom networks, financial services, ports, and public utilities are all exposed to risks that could disrupt daily life, undermine economic stability, and compromise national sovereignty.
As Bangladesh develops and embraces technology, the threat landscape expands just as quickly, and we must not only recognize this reality, but actively work to address it.
Cyberattacks on critical infrastructure are no longer rare globally but are becoming routine tools of geopolitical pressure, criminal extortion, and sabotage.
Indeed, Bangladesh’s own experience in recent years shows how vulnerable we are. The call for stronger defenses is therefore not merely prudent but necessary.
Protecting critical infrastructure of course is not only about technology but, much like any other issues, equally about governance. Without good governance, even the best tools will fail.
Our future development ambitions depend on stable, secure systems. Be it power, transport, banking, communications, or any other major sector, they all rely on infrastructure that must be defended against increasingly sophisticated threats.
We cannot wait for a crisis to expose its vulnerabilities and must instead act decisively and without delay to ensure these threats remain at bay for the safety and security of our people.

item.title: Better healthcare for a better nation
item.description:
The government’s plan to launch door‑to‑door healthcare services is exactly the kind of proactive, people‑centred initiative Bangladesh needs.
For too long, access to healthcare has depended on geography, income, and the ability to navigate overcrowded facilities. Taking essential services directly to citizens - especially the elderly, the poor, and those living with chronic illnesses - is a meaningful step toward equity.
This initiative recognizes a simple truth: Many Bangladeshis do not seek care because they cannot reach it. Distance, transport costs, long queues, and lost wages keep people away from clinics until their conditions worsen. A system that waits for patients to come forward is a system that leaves many behind.
As such, by sending trained health workers into communities, the government is shifting from reactive care to preventive care - and that shift will save lives.
The plan also aligns with Bangladesh’s broader public‑health needs. Non-communicable diseases are rising, and early detection of diabetes, hypertension, respiratory illnesses, and nutritional deficiencies can dramatically reduce long‑term costs and suffering. Door‑to‑door services can therefore bridge these gaps, offering screening, counselling, and referrals before problems escalate.
However, such an initiative only succeeds when it is implemented properly. While Health and Family Welfare Minister Sardar Md Sakhawat Husain spoke of a nationwide program, we hope that it is being piloted properly. Such a program will also be hollow if it fails to reach rural areas, urban slums, and marginalized communities.
It is also evident that Bangladesh needs more initiatives with this level of ambition and outreach. In addition, for healthcare in particular, such a project, while highly commendable, cannot replace community‑based healthcare needing expansion or local clinics strengthened.
Door‑to‑door healthcare can become a cornerstone of a more inclusive, resilient health system for our country, but only if it is scaled, funded, and sustained with seriousness.

item.title: How to solve groundwater depletion
item.description:
Bangladesh’s geography includes the extensive river deltas formed by major rivers like the Ganges and Brahmaputra, alongside a network of hundreds of small rivers. The monsoon season, occurring from June to September, contributes to 70-80% of the annual rainfall, while the dry winter months last from December to February. The annual rainfall distribution varies regionally, with the west receiving around 1,500 mm and the northeast and southeast receiving about 3,000 mm.
Despite being a country with numerous waterways and adequate rainfall, Bangladesh, unusually, faces severe water-related problems in different parts of the country due to low natural recharge and high water demand. The use of surface water has been in decline due to groundwater depletion and dependence on groundwater on the rise. The problem of managing groundwater in the drought-prone northwestern Barind tract is severe, where rainfall is low.
Currently, groundwater is vital for life and livelihoods, serving as the main source of clean drinking water and supporting agriculture. Approximately 90% of groundwater withdrawal is for agricultural use.
In densely populated regions, the demand for groundwater often exceeds the natural recharge rates, leading to a continual decline in water levels, a situation referred to as depletion.
This brings significant challenges, including dry wells, increased pumping costs, and reduced water availability for urban areas and agriculture. The situation intensifies food insecurity and water shortages, particularly as climate change results in more frequent droughts and altered rainfall patterns.
Against this backdrop, addressing groundwater depletion is critical. One strategy for mitigation involves using groundwater within safe yield limits, while another approach includes the artificial replenishment of groundwater aquifers.
In the Barind region, the Barind Integrated Area Development Project officially started in 1985 under the Bangladesh Agricultural Development Corporation. It was created to revitalize the impoverished by combating severe seasonal droughts, severe water scarcity, and the need for food security.
The project was reconstituted and upgraded into the Barind Multipurpose Development Authority in 1992. Groundwater issues were not mitigated and attention was given to the surface water bodies. However, groundwater problems need to be mitigated since 78% of BMDA projects rely on groundwater.
Coastal regions of Bangladesh suffer from severe salinity intrusion, exacerbated by sea-level rise and reduced river flow, rendering water unfit for consumption and agriculture, alongside arsenic and bacterial contamination, leading to health crises.
The managed aquifer recharge (MAR) technique is proposed as a practical solution to these groundwater issues across various regions of Bangladesh. MAR is a strategic approach aimed at intentionally replenishing aquifers with water for subsequent retrieval or enhancement of environmental conditions.
This method supplements natural groundwater recharge by either injecting or infiltrating water into the ground. Various techniques are employed in MAR, including the use of infiltration ponds, injection wells, and riverbank filtration.
Groundwater depletion is a significant issue in the Barind area, where the rate of decline ranges from 0.75 to 1.25 feet per year. This is exacerbated by limited natural aquifer recharge and high irrigation demands, leading to the ineffectiveness of some suction pumps.
To address depletion, two primary strategies are suggested: Utilizing groundwater within safe yield limits, which requires considerable time for aquifer recovery, and enhancing groundwater recharge through artificial recharge mechanisms.
Artificial recharge also offers additional benefits, such as mitigating salinity issues in coastal areas and managing water storage in urban centers like Dhaka. However, the disadvantages of MAR projects should also be taken into consideration before implementation.
Implementing a MAR project necessitates a conceptual understanding of aquifer characteristics, such as the presence and type of top aquitard (clay or silt), aquitard and aquifer thickness, hydraulic conductivity, transmissivity, and specific yield.
Properly designing a recharge well and assigning a recharge rate is critical; designing pumping and recharge wells involve different considerations. A low recharge rate is safer but prolongs the aquifer's recharge time, while a high rate risks clogging the aquifer, which occurs when pores are blocked, preventing adequate water flow if the rate exceeds hydraulic conductivity.
Therefore, careful site specific design of recharge rates and well spacing is essential. The success of a MAR project fully depends on appropriate site specific design of the project.
Water quality is also a significant concern, particularly if contaminated water is used, as it can detrimentally damage aquifers. The preferred water sources rank as follows: 1) rainwater (low contaminated), 2) pond and lake water (moderately contaminated), and 3) river water, which in Bangladesh is compromised by urban pollution.
Studies indicate river water quality varies from fair to marginal, with high levels of sulphate and contamination risks like arsenic and salinity. Thus, river water must be treated to remove contaminants before it can be safely injected into the aquifer.
Dr. Mohammad Abul Fazal is a specialist in agricultural engineering. He achieved his PhD in groundwater management from Kyoto University, Japan.

Oil passes $100 a barrel again: Why it's more serious this time
item.description:
Brent crude went back over $100 a barrel Thursday for the first time since May, as the conflict in the Middle East intensified.
This time, however, the impact on the world economy could be greater given the conflict shows no sign of ending and reserves have already been partially been drawn down.
The last time Brent crude -- the benchmark international oil contact -- broke above $100 a barrel was in March 12 during the first stage or the US-Israeli war on Iran.
The last time before that was when Russia launched its invasion of Ukraine in 2022.
This latest price surge has been fuelled in part by attacks on Saudi Arabian tankers in the Red Sea by pro-Iranian Houthi rebel forces in Yemen, which would expand the zone of conflict and could cut off the export of millions of barrels of oil.
In normal circumstances, the world economy consumes a little over 100 million barrels of oil daily.
In mid-July, the International Energy Agency (IEA) warned that world production was below pre-war levels by around 9.4 million barrels a day.
The threat to the Red Sea raises the stakes.
It is the alternative route that Saudi Arabia has been using since the Strait of Hormuz was closed to export its oil.
After the war began it stepped up shipments via pipeline to the Red Sea port of Yanbu, which has allowed it export some three quarters of its pre-war level.
"This workaround is now facing headwinds as the blockade and the threat of attacks on ships will make passage through the Red Sea less viable in the near-term," said Maya Senussi of economic advisory firm Oxford Economics.
Oxford Economics fears that if both the Red Sea and the Strait of Hormuz were effectively closed to traffic, the price of oil could eventually pass $160 a barrel.
"Much of the world's spare production capacity has already been used, while strategic and commercial oil inventories are lower than when the war began, leaving the market with fewer buffers against a prolonged supply disruption," said Janiv Shah, vice president of the Rysted Energy consultancy.
That leaves the markets with less room for manoeuvre, he pointed out.
On Tuesday, the IEA warned that the renewed fighting in the Middle East was increasing concerns over energy supplies.
But there were still cushioning factors, it added.
It cited increased exports from several countries and Saudi Arabia and United Arab Emirates oil reaching markets by alternative routes.
Producers including the Brazil, Kazakhstan, United States and Venezuela were increasing their production, and China was trying to cut its imports, it added.
And around 30 IEA countries had between more than a billion barrels of oil in reserve.
Earlier this month the Kpler consultancy calculated there was a record 1.35 billion barrels of "oil on water" -- product already at sea.
That helps limit further crude oil prices rises, it argued.
The head of French oil giant TotalEnergies was similarly phlegmatic when he announced the company's second quarter results Thursday.
"We have no problem with stocks to supply our refineries," he said.
European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde nevertheless expressed alarm Thursday at the Houthi attack on a Saudi vessel in the Red Sea.
The situation in the Red Sea "is clearly going to have an impact and is having an impact", she said. "We can see that on the price of Brent as it evolves almost by the hour."
And some of the bank's governors had already mooted a possible rates hike, she added.
Inflationary pressure could encourage central banks to maintain high rates or even raise them.
Energy costs, not just of oil but also of gas in winter, hit both production and consumption and have a knock-on effect on economic growth.

Confidence rising as Sooryavanshi reflects on historic knock
item.description:
India opener Vaibhav Sooryavanshi said his confidence continues to grow after becoming the youngest player to score a half-century in international cricket, following a breathtaking 18-ball fifty against Zimbabwe in the series opener in Harare.
When his assault concluded, his 50-run knock included four fours and as many sixes off just 19 balls.
Speaking after the match, the opener said his early success at the highest level has given him added belief.
"When it's the starting phase of your career, and you get these kinds of innings early on, and you perform well, it builds confidence for the upcoming matches," Sooryavanshi was quoted as saying to BCCI.tv.
"It felt like I was in my zone and I just backed my strengths. That was all."
Despite the individual milestone, Sooryavanshi was quick to acknowledge the people who have played a role in his journey so far.
"I would like to dedicate this (fifty) to my family, my coaches, and to everyone who has supported me along my journey, through every good and bad phase."
The youngster also revealed the story behind his celebration with his hands after reaching his half-century.
"I did that celebration for my mum, and the other one was based on a conversation with Rinku (Singh) bhaiya."
Sooryavanshi's latest innings continued his impressive record at the venue.
The southpaw slammed a record-breaking 175 off just 80 deliveries earlier this year at the Harare Sports Club, leading India to the title at the ICC Men's Under-19 World Cup.
The youngster admitted he enjoys batting there while remaining focused on contributing to the team wherever he plays.
"You could say that I like the vibe here," he said.
"There are some grounds where you just love scoring runs. I'm not taking this for granted at all. I'm just trying to give my best, contribute to the team, perform well wherever I play."
The second T20I between India and Zimbabwe is scheduled for Saturday, July 25 at the same venue.
Series schedule:
First T20I: India won by seven wickets
Second T20I: July 25, Harare Sports Club
Third T20I: July 26, Harare Sports Club

item.title: Brian Lara Stadium hosts maiden Test as West Indies face Pakistan
item.description:
West Indies and Pakistan meet at Test cricket's newest venue in Trinidad on Saturday at the start of a two-match series in which the home side will be enjoying rare favoured status against the unsettled visitors.
Brian Lara Stadium, located in the south of the island, has previously hosted one-day and T20 internationals along with regional first-class fixtures.
It becomes the nation's second Test match venue and 13th in the West Indies, drawing it alongside Queen's Park Oval in the capital, Port of Spain, the venue of the second Test a week later.
Roston Chase enjoyed his first match and series wins as West Indies Test captain just three weeks ago when the Caribbean team demolished Sri Lanka by an innings and 217 runs and then played out a draw in the second and final Test, both matches played in Antigua.
Chase missed the recent ODI series loss to New Zealand with an injury to his right index finger.
"Roston is a fighter. I know he's not going to want to miss any Test cricket," West Indies head coach Daren Sammy said.
"I'm pretty sure Dr. Byam (team physio Denis Byam) has a way of keeping guys on the park, so I'm pretty sure Roston will be ready for the first Test."
It was their first home Test series triumph since a 2-0 sweep of Bangladesh in 2022, a result which also gave them their last consecutive series wins anywhere as the whitewash of the Bangladeshis was preceded by a notable 1-0 home triumph over England in a three-Test contest.
West Indies are not expected to make significant alterations to the winning combination from the Sri Lankan series, although left-arm spinner Jomel Warrican is likely to come into consideration at the expense of fast-medium bowler Anderson Phillip given the expectation of a pitch favouring spin later in the match.
- In-form Ali -
In stark contrast, Pakistan have once again revamped their squad in the aftermath of a 2-0 series loss in Bangladesh two months ago with senior batsman Babar Azam returning as captain in place of Shan Masood in hope of reviving their Test fortunes.
Babar and opening batsman Azan Awais both compiled hundreds in the lone warm-up match against a West Indies Select XI at the weekend while most of the other main batsmen also got confidence-boosting runs.
However the most encouraging aspect of the match in batting-friendly conditions for the tourists was the bowling of pacer Mohammad Ali, who took five wickets in the first innings and will be relied upon, together with the other members of the restructured pace armoury, to give Pakistan a chance to end a miserable run of seven consecutive Test match losses away from home.
Their last visit to the Caribbean in 2021 finished in a 1-1 draw after two Tests in Kingston while the teams' most recent Test encounters were in the spin-dominant Multan in January of 2025 when the West Indies trumped Pakistan at their own game in the second match to also leave that series at 1-1.
This meeting of two unpredictable sides carries the additional dimension of intervention by the elements as the July/August period is usually one of significant rainfall in the southern Caribbean.
While Trinidad has enjoyed hot, sunny conditions in the days leading up to the first Test, there is certainly no guarantee that such pleasant cricketing weather will be sustained over the fortnight of this short series.

item.title: Italy approaches Guardiola, Ancelotti as search for new national coach intensifies
item.description:
The Italian Football Federation (FIGC) has held discussions with former Manchester City manager Pep Guardiola and Brazil head coach Carlo Ancelotti as it looks to appoint a new national team coach following Italy's latest World Cup disappointment.
The federation hopes to name a new coach within the week after the Azzurri failed to qualify for the FIFA World Cup for a third consecutive time.
Former Italy captain and AC Milan legend Paolo Maldini, who was appointed FIGC technical director earlier this month, confirmed on Thursday that both Guardiola and Ancelotti had been approached.
Speaking after a meeting with Serie A clubs alongside new FIGC president Giovanni Malagò and special adviser Leonardo, Maldini said the federation initially contacted Ancelotti before opening talks with Guardiola.
"We wanted to begin by speaking with the best coaches in the world to understand whether they would be interested," Maldini said, while declining to provide further details on the negotiations.
Guardiola left Manchester City at the end of last season after a highly successful 10-year spell in which he guided the club to numerous domestic and international titles. Over his managerial career with Barcelona, Bayern Munich and City, the 55-year-old has won 35 major trophies.
According to Maldini, he and Leonardo recently travelled to Barcelona for discussions with Guardiola about the Italy job.
He said the federation aims to appoint the preferred candidate as soon as possible but is prepared to wait if necessary.
Ancelotti, meanwhile, recently extended his contract with Brazil through the 2030 World Cup. However, Brazil endured a disappointing campaign, exiting the tournament in the round of 16 following a 2-1 defeat to Norway.
The 67-year-old Italian is one of football's most decorated coaches, having won league titles in Italy, England, France, Spain and Germany, along with a record five UEFA Champions League trophies.
Italy's latest failure to qualify for the World Cup came after a playoff defeat to Bosnia and Herzegovina in March. The setback prompted the resignations of FIGC president Gabriele Gravina and national team coach Gennaro Gattuso.
Gattuso had taken charge only last June after replacing Luciano Spalletti, whose tenure ended following Italy's poor start to the qualifying campaign.
Former Italy managers Roberto Mancini, who led the Azzurri to the UEFA Euro 2021 title, and Antonio Conte are also being considered for the role. Former World Cup-winning midfielder Andrea Pirlo has likewise been linked with the vacancy.

item.title: King Charles opens slimmed down Commonwealth Games in Glasgow
item.description:
King Charles III officially opened the 23rd Commonwealth Games in Glasgow on Thursday to launch 10 days in a slimmed down event designed to control costs.
Around 3,000 athletes from 74 nations and territories will compete for 215 gold medals across 10 sports and six para-sports in the Scottish city, which also hosted the games 12 years ago.
Accompanied by his wife Queen Camilla, the King emerged from a Tardis, inspired by British science fiction TV show Doctor Who, to get the opening ceremony off to a jovial start before taking his seat in the 14,000-capacity Hydro arena in the centre of the city.
"As we gather, we celebrate the dedication and talent of every athlete and team. Your perseverance inspires us, and we eagerly anticipate your achievements in the coming days," King Charles said addressing the athletes.
"As we share our love of sport, and our planet, let us build a harmonious and durable future, for our families and each other.
"It is now my great pleasure to declare the twenty-third Commonwealth Games open."
A first ever indoor opening ceremony for the event, which originated as the British Empire Games in 1930 and is still made up mainly of countries once subject to British imperial rule, is part of a major restructuring of the Games which were on the brink of collapse three years ago.
The Australian state of Victoria withdrew as hosts of the event citing spiralling costs in 2023.
Glasgow stepped in to fill the breach and keep the Commonwealth Games project on track, but at a quarter of the cost when it hosted 12 years ago and with the sporting programme nearly halved from 19 sports in Birmingham four years ago.
"When the future of these Games was uncertain, Glasgow didn't ask, 'Can we?', We asked, 'When do we get started?'" George Black, Chair of Glasgow 2026, said.
"Because that's what we do. We roll up our sleeves and, together, we make extraordinary things happen."
The indoor venue allowed for the athletes to take to the stage as they were welcomed into the arena with new UK Prime Minister Andy Burnham among those in attendance, alongside Scotland's First Minister John Swinney.
The Falkland Islands' six-strong team were among those to receive the biggest ovation in the wake of the fallout from Argentina's World Cup victory over England when Argentine players paraded a banner reading: "Las Malvinas son Argentinas" (The Falklands are Argentine).
Argentina invaded the British Overseas Territory in the South Atlantic in 1982, but Britain regained the archipelago in a short-lived war.
Athletics and swimming lead the bill alongside artistic gymnastics, track cycling, netball, weightlifting, boxing, judo, bowls and 3x3 basketball.
The largest ever para-sport programme at a Commonwealth Games will include athletics, swimming, track cycling, powerlifting, bowls and wheelchair basketball.
Fresh from breaking a 27-year world record for the mile on Saturday, Scotland's Josh Kerr is the poster boy for his home games.
Pakistan's Olympic champion Arshad Nadeem's showdown for javelin gold with India's Neeraj Chopra is another highlights of the athletics programme.
England's Adam Peaty goes for gold twice in the 50 and 100m breaststroke at the site where he won his first international medals 12 years ago, while South African Chad Le Clos aims to become the most decorated male athlete in Commonwealth history by adding to his 18 medals.

item.title: Barcelona's De Jong returns from World Cup with torn ligament
item.description:
Barcelona's Dutch midfielder Frenkie de Jong is undergoing treatment on a torn knee ligament suffered while on national duty at the World Cup, the Spanish champions said on Thursday.
De Jong said the injury came to light on his return from the World Cup when Barcelona doctors returned a more serious diagnosis than the original assessment from the Dutch medical staff.
De Jong suffered "a tear of the medial collateral ligament (MCL) in his right knee," Barcelona said in a statement.
"It has been agreed that the player will continue with conservative treatment under the supervision of the club's medical staff. His progress will be monitored over the coming weeks."
De Jong said he played through the pain at the World Cup where the Netherlands exited in the last-32.
"During the World Cup I injured my knee. After the first assessments, the (Dutch team) doctors told me it was a small injury and that it would not get worse if I continued playing," De Jong said on Instagram.
"The only challenge was playing with some pain, but throughout my career I have done that whenever I could contribute to the team, both for my club and my country."
After starting in two group phase matches De Jong, 29, missed the Netherlands' third game, a 3-1 win over Tunisia, but played 109 minutes of the penalty shoot-out defeat to Morocco in the first knock-out round.
The situation changed when he returned to club duties, where more intensive medical tests revealed the true extent of the damage.
"These showed that the injury was more serious than initially determined. Thankfully at this stage there is no need for surgery and I am now fully focused on my recovery and returning to the pitch as soon as possible," he added.
Barcelona begin the defence of their Liga title away at Elche on August 23.

item.title: Modric signs new contract with AC Milan
item.description:
Luka Modric will continue his playing career with AC Milan, as the Croatia veteran signed a new contract with the Rossoneri, the club confirmed on Thursday.
Modric, who turns 41 in September, joined AC Milan last year after 13 seasons with Real Madrid. His original contract had expired on June 30.
He had previously stated he would make a decision on whether to stay at the club or retire after the 2026 World Cup, where Croatia lost to Portugal and exited in the round of 32.
"AC Milan is pleased to announce that Luka Modric has signed a contract for a further season, until June 30, 2027," read a club statement.
"A key figure in the Rossoneri midfield since his arrival, Luka has brought his experience and quality to the service of the team, both on and off the pitch, with humility and great passion. One of the finest and most iconic players in world football, the Croatian will continue his journey in the Rossoneri shirt - with which he made 37 appearances and scored two goals last season - with the ambition to reach new, important milestones."
The 2018 Ballon d'Or winner will continue to wear the No. 14 shirt next season.

item.title: Casemiro joins Inter Miami amid tampering probe
item.description:
Inter Miami said Wednesday it completed the signing of Brazilian midfielder Casemiro as a free agent, adding one of the biggest names in world football to a squad that already features Lionel Messi.
The announcement was followed by news that the MLS had launched an investigation into alleged irregularities involving the move.
Inter Miami said the 34-year-old, who left Manchester United in June, agreed to a contract that runs until the end of 2027, with an option to extend to June 2029.
Casemiro confirmed in January that he and United had mutually decided to part ways after four years. In its statement, Inter Miami said it had obtained Casemiro's discovery rights from LA Galaxy as part of the transfer.
Under MLS rules, a club may place up to five players on a discovery list, giving it first option to sign any of them ahead of other league clubs. Galaxy had held that priority over Casemiro, meaning Inter Miami needed to reach an agreement with the Californian club before it could pursue him.
Casemiro won five Champions League titles at Real Madrid before joining Manchester United in 2022, going on to help the club lift the League Cup and FA Cup. He has earned 91 caps for Brazil and started all five of Brazil's matches at this year's World Cup before the team's round of 16 exit.
Inter Miami co-owner David Beckham welcomed the signing, calling Casemiro "a winner who has achieved so much in the game" over his career at Real Madrid and Manchester United. Casemiro himself said in the club's statement: "What motivates me the most, and I think this is true for every player, is winning and continuing to grow."
MLS said Inter Miami and LA Galaxy had reached a settlement over Casemiro's discovery rights, though the terms of that agreement will not be made public until the tampering investigation concludes.
LA Galaxy confirmed the settlement in a statement of its own, saying the terms "will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation currently open with Major League Soccer."
MLS said: "The league is gathering all relevant information and will refrain from further comment until the review is complete," adding, "While Inter Miami CF and the LA Galaxy have reached a settlement for the discovery priority [first rights] to sign Casemiro, the terms will be released upon conclusion of the tampering investigation."
Under MLS regulations, tampering covers any attempt by a club to negotiate with or make an offer to a player under contract with, or subject to the priority rights of, another club, without league clearance.
It is not the first time Inter Miami's transfer dealings have drawn league scrutiny.
The club was fined 2 million U.S. dollars in 2021 after MLS found it had breached roster rules in its signing of French midfielder Blaise Matuidi, effectively giving the team four "designated players" rather than the permitted three under a rule introduced when Beckham himself joined LA Galaxy in 2007.

item.title: Teenage sensation Sooryavanshi's 18-ball fifty powers India to T20 win over Zimbabwe
item.description:
Fifteen-year-old Vaibhav Sooryavanshi produced a stunning batting display, smashing his maiden international half-century as India cruised to a seven-wicket victory over Zimbabwe in the opening Twenty20 match on Thursday.
Chasing a modest target of 126, Sooryavanshi blasted 50 off just 18 deliveries before falling for 68, having top-edged an attempted cut shot off Richard Ngarava. His explosive innings all but sealed the contest for India, which reached 126-3 with captain Shreyas Iyer remaining unbeaten to register his first victory as skipper in eight T20 internationals.
Earlier, Zimbabwe managed 125-7 after being put under pressure by India's bowlers, with Mayank Yadav and Prince Yadav claiming two wickets each.
Sooryavanshi, who earlier this month became India's youngest-ever international cricketer, had a quiet start to his international career during the T20 series in England, scoring 14, 13 and 15 before being dropped for the final match, which India also lost as England completed a 4-0 series sweep.
The selectors retained faith in the teenage opener for the Zimbabwe series, returning him to the same Harare venue where he had earlier announced himself by hammering 175 off 80 balls against England in the ICC Under-19 World Cup final in February.
The youngster wasted little time on Thursday, launching his first six after facing just four deliveries. He went on to clear the boundary three more times with towering hits over long-off, fine leg and long-on.
The three-match series will continue with the second T20 on Saturday.

item.title: Mustafizur to miss entire The Hundred season due to hamstring injury
item.description:
Bangladesh pace spearhead Mustafizur Rahman will miss the entire 2026 season of The Hundred in England after suffering a hamstring injury.
Mustafizur was signed by Birmingham Phoenix for this year's tournament, which began on July 21. However, the franchise confirmed in a Facebook post yesterday that the left-arm pacer has been ruled out for the season because of the injury.
Popularly known as the "Cutter Master," Mustafizur was picked by Birmingham Phoenix for his base price of 100,000 pounds at the player auction held in March.
He sustained the hamstring injury during the first ODI against Zimbabwe earlier this month after bowling six overs. The injury also forced him to miss the remaining two matches of the three-match ODI series.
Birmingham Phoenix are yet to announce a replacement for the Bangladesh fast bowler.

item.title: When growing old means growing invisible
Protection is a life skill. Why aren’t we teaching it?
Helping our footwear industry fulfil its potential
Dhaka air quality moderate Friday morning
item.description:
Dhaka, the capital of Bangladesh, recorded moderate air quality on Friday morning, ranking 41st among cities with the worst air pollution worldwide.
At 9:50am, the city's Air Quality Index (AQI) stood at 66, indicating moderate air quality, according to air quality monitoring platform IQAir.
A moderate AQI means the air quality is generally acceptable. However, people who are unusually sensitive to air pollution may experience minor to moderate health effects if exposed for prolonged periods.
Kinshasa in the Democratic Republic of the Congo topped the list of the world's most polluted cities with an AQI score of 166, followed by Kampala, Uganda, with a score of 164.
Both cities recorded air quality classified as "unhealthy."
The AQI is calculated based on the concentration of major air pollutants, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), nitrogen dioxide (NO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), sulphur dioxide (SO₂) and ozone (O₃).
According to the AQI scale, a score between 51 and 100 is considered moderate, while 101–150 is deemed unhealthy for sensitive groups, 151–200 is unhealthy, 201–300 is very unhealthy, and 301 or above is classified as hazardous, posing serious health risks to the entire population.
Dhaka has long struggled with air pollution, with air quality typically deteriorating during the dry winter months and improving during the monsoon season due to rainfall and stronger winds.
The World Health Organization estimates that air pollution causes around seven million deaths globally each year, mainly due to stroke, heart disease, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, lung cancer and acute respiratory infections.

Mozaffar shown arrested in crimes against humanity case
item.description:
The International Crimes Tribunal (ICT) on Thursday ordered that Major (retd) Mozaffar Hossain, one of the prime accused in the former president Ziaur Rahman assassination case, to be shown arrested in a case over crimes against humanity committed in Feni during the July 2024 uprising.
The three-member ICT-2, chaired by Justice Nazrul Islam Chowdhury, also granted the prosecution two days to interrogate the accused.
It fixed September 23 for submission of the investigation report.
The other members of the tribunal are Judge Md Manjurul Bashid and Judge Nur Mohammad Shahriar Kabir.
Chief Prosecutor Md Aminul Islam represented the prosecution during the hearing.
Earlier in the day, Mozaffar was produced before the tribunal. He will remain in Dhaka Cantonment Jail.
The Detective Branch (DB) of Dhaka Metropolitan Police (DMP) arrested Mozaffar in the capital on July 15 after over 45 years on the run.
Later, he was handed over to the Military Police at Dhaka Cantonment as his trial in the Zia assassination case will be held under the military law.
Meanwhile, another accused in the crimes against humanity case, Lieutenant General (retd) Masud Uddin Chowdhury, was also produced before the tribunal.

item.title: Experts push Volunteer Peer Leader model for adolescent health
item.description:
A national consultation recommended institutionalizing the Volunteer Peer Leader (VPL) Model within Adolescent-Friendly Health Centers (AFHCs) to improve adolescents' access to inclusive, youth-friendly health services, particularly mental health support.
The consultation, organized by SERAC-Bangladesh, brought together policymakers, public health experts, development partners, youth representatives and government officials to discuss integrating the model into the country's public health system.
The meeting was chaired by Dr Syed Kamrul Islam, director (PHC & ITHC) at the Directorate General of Health Services (DGHS) and chair of the Technical Working Group.
In his welcome remarks, SM Shaikat, executive director of SERAC-Bangladesh, said: "The Volunteer Peer Leader Model offers an effective solution to the human resource shortages in health facilities."
He said trained Volunteer Peer Leaders provide basic counseling, essential adolescent health information and referral support, serving as a bridge between communities and health facilities through a government-approved training curriculum.
Md Nazmul Hasan, project manager of SERAC-Bangladesh, presented the rationale for the VPL Model, its key interventions and gaps in adolescent health services. He said the model improves adolescents' access to health information, basic counseling, referral services and adolescent-friendly healthcare.
During the discussion, participants said institutionalizing the model would require a clear implementation framework, well-defined terms of reference, policy endorsement and adequate financial planning.
They also noted that many adolescents remain reluctant to seek healthcare because of social and cultural barriers, adding that trained Volunteer Peer Leaders can provide counseling, health education, awareness and referrals while strengthening links between communities and health facilities.
Participants said initiatives are underway to establish Adolescent Focal Bodies at the upazila and district levels in line with the National Adolescent Health Strategy.
They added that integrating the VPL Model into this structure, supported by standardized training packages, implementation guidelines, policy briefs and financial analysis, would facilitate its adoption within the government health system. Gradual expansion of the model across all upazilas, they said, could improve adolescents' access to inclusive and adolescent-friendly health services.
Sharing field experiences, Volunteer Peer Leader Md Boni Amin from Barguna said VPLs receive foundation training on the National Adolescent Health Strategy, adolescent development, communication skills and group facilitation before conducting awareness sessions in schools and communities, encouraging adolescents to seek healthcare and facilitating referrals to health facilities.
The consultation was attended by senior officials from the Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, DGHS, the Directorate General of Family Planning (DGFP), development partners, academics, members of the Technical Working Group, Volunteer Peer Leaders, representatives of government and nongovernment organizations, and members of the national media.

item.title: KU students lock admin building, boycott classes over hidden camera allegation
item.description:
Khulna University (KU) students on Thursday locked the administrative building and suspended all academic activities, demanding justice over allegations that a hidden mobile phone was used to secretly record videos inside a women's dormitory washroom.
The protest erupted after residents of Bijoy ’24 Hall discovered a mobile phone concealed inside a washroom on Wednesday night.
Students subsequently detained Imam Hasan, 24, son of the hall canteen owner, who had been working at the canteen for about two years. They accused him of secretly recording videos of female students.
According to students, the phone was spotted around 10:30pm, prompting suspicion and a search. They alleged that several secretly recorded videos were found on the device.
The protesters further claimed that Imam had been recording videos through a washroom ventilator for an extended period and sharing them in various Telegram groups. They also alleged that his wife was aware of the activities.
The incident sparked outrage among residential students, who raised concerns about their privacy and safety.
Students held a protest rally at Hadi Chattar at noon, demanding a fair investigation and exemplary punishment for those responsible.
They alleged that the university administration had failed to take effective action hours after the incident came to light and claimed that previous allegations of sexual harassment involving university teachers had also not been adequately addressed.
The protesters declared that all academic and administrative activities would remain suspended until a credible investigation is completed and those responsible are brought to justice.
Bijoy ’24 Hall Provost Prof Dr Selina Ahmed said the matter is being investigated.
“The investigation is ongoing. It would not be appropriate to comment before it is completed. Necessary information and actions will be shared after the investigation report is received,” she said.

item.title: Bandarban residents risk lives crossing flood-damaged bridge
item.description:
Thousands of residents in Kuhalong Union of Bandarban Sadar are enduring severe hardship as a bridge over the Camlong stream, damaged by repeated floods, remains unsafe and unusable for vehicles.
The bridge, which first collapsed during the devastating floods of 2019, has never been fully repaired. As a result, residents—including students from at least five educational institutions—have been forced to cross the damaged structure daily, risking their lives due to the lack of an alternative route.
According to local sources, Kuhalong Union is home to more than 13,000 people. Around 5,000 residents of Camlong Para, Bhangamura Para, Barua Para, Marma Para, Dhalu Para, Chemir Mukh and Goalia Khola rely on the bridge to travel to Bandarban town and transport fruits and vegetables to markets in Chittagong and other areas.
The recent floods, triggered by days of heavy rainfall across Bandarban, have further weakened the bridge. Large sections are broken, while layers of mud deposited by floodwaters have made even pedestrian movement hazardous.
“The bridge has become a death trap. People have been suffering for years, but we have no other way to travel,” said Thuy Nu Ching Marma, a resident of Camlong area.
Local jeep driver Md Alam said the bridge had remained damaged for years and was further battered by the latest floods.
“Vehicles can no longer cross the bridge safely, disrupting transport and trade in the area,” he said.
Another resident, Md Jahangir, expressed frustration over the prolonged delay in rebuilding the bridge despite its importance to thousands of people.
Kuhalong Union Parishad Chairman Mongpu Marma said the bridge's collapse has severely disrupted the movement of people and goods. He added that the matter has repeatedly been brought to the attention of the relevant authorities.
Responding to the concerns, LGED Bandarban Executive Engineer Pratipada Dewan said a contractor has already been appointed to construct a new bridge over the Camlong stream at a cost of Tk 5 crore.
“The project has been approved and construction work will begin soon. Once completed, the new bridge will significantly improve communication and transportation in the area,” he said.

item.title: Over 800 acres of cropland in Thakurgaon remain submerged for months
item.description:
More than 800 acres of agricultural land at Salandar Farm under Thakurgaon Sugar Mill have remained submerged for nearly four months, causing extensive crop losses and pushing hundreds of farmers toward financial hardship.
Farmers say prolonged waterlogging has damaged sugarcane, potato, rice and other seasonal crops on around 814 acres of leased farmland, threatening both local livelihoods and a key source of agricultural seed production.
According to farm authorities, 33 farmers cultivated sugarcane on 239 acres this season, while another 581 acres were used for various rabi and kharif crops. Most of the affected cultivators are landless or low-income farmers who lease land from the government-owned farm through annual tenders.
Farmers estimate that sugarcane cultivation costs around Tk115,000 per acre, with many relying on loans from sugar mills and non-governmental organizations to finance production.
Lutfar Rahman, a leaseholder at the farm, said his entire sugarcane field had been ruined after remaining under water for months.
“I borrowed money to cultivate sugarcane, but the crop has rotted due to prolonged waterlogging. Now I do not know how I will repay the loans,” he said.
Many other farmers reported similar losses, saying potato fields, rice seedlings and other crops had also been destroyed.
Farmers and local residents blame the situation on a neglected drainage canal that once carried excess water from the farm to the Dhepa River. They allege that years of silt accumulation, weed growth and lack of maintenance have blocked the canal, preventing water from draining even after moderate rainfall.
“Previously, rainwater from the farm flowed into the Dhepa River through the canal. Since that connection was lost, even a small amount of rainfall causes extensive waterlogging,” said Jalal Uddin, a farmer from the Sotapir area.
Despite repeated appeals, affected farmers say no effective steps have been taken to resolve the problem.
Agricultural experts and local stakeholders warn that the impact extends beyond individual farmers. Salandar Farm supplies sugarcane to Thakurgaon Sugar Mill and produces foundation and certified seeds of crops including potato, rice, chilli and pulses for distribution across the country.
Abdul Mannan, an agriculturist and leaseholder, described the situation as a “man-made disaster”.
“Regular maintenance and re-excavation of the drainage canal could have prevented this crisis. Farmers informed the authorities long ago, but no effective action has been taken,” he said.
Farmers estimate that nearly 1,000 acres of surrounding farmland outside the farm area are also affected by waterlogging. They believe re-excavating the canal and restoring its connection to the Dhepa River could protect around 2,000 acres of fertile land and significantly increase agricultural output.
The affected farmers have submitted a memorandum to the district administration, demanding urgent excavation of the canal and immediate measures to resolve the long-standing waterlogging problem. They warn that without swift intervention, both the local sugar industry and the country's seed production network could face serious setbacks.
Former Managing Director of Thakurgaon Sugar Mill Shahjahan Kabir said the authorities are concerned about the waterlogging situation at Salandar Farm.
“We are concerned about the persistent waterlogging at Salandar Farm. Initiatives are being taken to renovate the drainage canal as quickly as possible in coordination with the Bangladesh Water Development Board and the district administration,” he said.
Acting Managing Director Saiful Islam said he had only recently assumed office.
“I have just taken charge. After reviewing the matter, I will discuss it with all relevant stakeholders and take the necessary steps,” he said.

item.title: BNP leader Rafiqul Islam Miah on life support
item.description:
BNP Standing Committee member and former minister Rafiqul Islam Miah was placed on life support at Evercare Hospital Dhaka on Thursday evening after his condition deteriorated.
He was put on life support at 7:20pm, his Personal Secretary Muksedur Rahman Abir said.
He said the BNP leader’s kidneys are no longer functioning, and doctors detected blood infection. "He has been suffering from neurological problems for a long time and is now facing multiple complications," he added.
Rafiqul Islam was admitted to Evercare Hospital on July 21 and had been receiving treatment in a cabin equipped with ICU facilities. However, his condition began to worsen from around noon on Thursday, his personal secretary said.
He is currently under the care of specialist physician Dr Farid Hossain at the hospital.
Abir said the family has sought prayers from people across the country for Rafiqul Islam's recovery.

item.title: Bangladesh expects Delhi to address peacefully protests in India
item.description:
State Minister for Foreign Affairs Shama Obaed Islam on Thursday said Bangladesh expects India to address promptly and peacefully the concerns of its people, being the world’s largest democracy.
"As a democratic government and state, we would expect India, as the world's largest democracy, to take into account the position of its people and move quickly towards resolving the problem," she told a media briefing at the foreign office.
The junior minister made the comments while responding to a question on the current student-led protests in the neighbouring country, adding “Bangladesh wants peace and stability to prevail in the region”.
Asked for comments on media reports about President Mohammed Shahabuddin’s alleged telephone conversation with deposed prime minister Sheikh Hasina, she said the foreign ministry had not yet any specific information on the matter.
Shama, however, said the government would investigate the matter if any relevant information became available.

item.title: Call for greater awareness to protect women's mental and physical health
item.description:
Speakers at a seminar on Saturday stressed the need to ensure women's mental and physical well-being as a prerequisite for building a healthy society, calling for greater public awareness and easier access to modern healthcare services.
The seminar, titled "Protection of Women's Mental and Physical Health," was organised by the Bangladesh Nari Sangbadik Samity (BANASAS) at the National Press Club on Wednesday.
The chief guest, Prof Dr Sheikh Nazmul Huda, chairman of the Department of Public Health at the International University of Business Agriculture and Technology (IUBAT), highlighted the indications, benefits and potential risks of Caesarean section (C-section).
He also discussed the role of political decisions and public policies in shaping healthcare services.
He said hysterectomy is not always necessary for young women suffering from conditions such as fibroids, cysts or tumours, noting that advances in modern medical treatment often make it possible to preserve the uterus while providing effective care.
Former Associate Professor of Shaheed Suhrawardy Medical College and Hospital, Dr Yasmin Jahan Shaheen, called for a nationwide drive to expand cervical cancer screening, increase uptake of the human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccine and strengthen public awareness, warning that stigma and late diagnosis continue to claim preventable lives.
Dr Shaheen noted that cervical cancer usually develops gradually over a decade and often remains symptom-free in its early stages. She said the disease is largely preventable through HPV vaccination, ideally administered between the ages of 9 and 14, before exposure to the virus.
She added that treatment varies depending on the patient's condition and the stage of the disease, while early detection is possible through the simple Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid (VIA) screening test, which helps identify precancerous changes and enables timely treatment.
Zakia Shishir, convener of the Agrajatra Nari Forum and a human rights activist, said families and society have a critical role to play in safeguarding women's mental health, as women continue to face domestic violence, workplace harassment, social bullying and cyber abuse on a regular basis.
‘Such violence and abuse often leave women struggling with depression and psychological distress, adversely affecting their work, thinking and future aspirations. It also undermines their self-confidence, hindering both their personal and professional development.
Pharmacist and researcher Samin Fardeen Soumik delivered a presentation on osteoporosis, describing it as a "silent" bone disease that gradually weakens bones, leaving them fragile and prone to fractures from even minor falls or routine daily activities.
He said women are at greater risk of developing osteoporosis after menopause due to hormonal changes. Smoking, excessive alcohol consumption, physical inactivity and a family history of the disease are among the major risk factors.
“Osteoporosis can largely be prevented through a healthy diet, regular exercise, maintaining a healthy weight, avoiding smoking, ensuring adequate intake of calcium and vitamin D, and limiting excessive salt consumption,” he added.
Among others, Zauhar Iqbal Khan, general secretary of the Dhaka Sub-Editors Council; Shahjahan Swapan, welfare secretary of the Dhaka Union of Journalists; and Nahid Hasan, sports and cultural secretary of the Dhaka Sub-Editors Council, also addressed the seminar.
The programme was chaired by Nasima Akter Soma, President of the Bangladesh Nari Sangbadik Samity (BANASAS).

item.title: Rizvi questions who sheltered Zia assassination convict for 45 years
Info minister: Authority needed to identify journalists
item.description:
Information and Broadcasting Minister Zahir Uddin Swapon on Thursday said there is a need for an appropriate authority to determine who qualifies as a journalist and who does not.
The minister made the remarks while delivering a directive speech at the 34th meeting of the Press Accreditation Committee and the inaugural meeting of the newly formed committee, held at the conference room of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting at the Bangladesh Secretariat in the capital.
He advised members of the Press Accreditation Committee to ensure that every decision is consistent with the Constitution, existing laws, and relevant rules and regulations.
He urged them to exercise foresight, wisdom, and adopt an inclusive approach that accommodates the largest possible number of people while making decisions.
He also reminded the committee that its decisions should be broadly acceptable.
The minister said committee members must work not as representatives of any political party but as responsible representatives of a government elected by the people, bearing equal responsibility toward all citizens.
He said the media is a pluralistic sector where there is no alternative to the coexistence of people with diverse opinions, perspectives, and ideas. Therefore, instead of making narrow or biased decisions, the committee should adopt an inclusive approach that accommodates the largest possible number of people.
"The true capability of political leadership lies in making acceptable and inclusive decisions, even in difficult and sensitive situations," the minister said.
Zahir Uddin Swapon said the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting is one of the state's most politically sensitive ministries. Even a minor wrong decision can have major political and social consequences in the future. Therefore, decisions should be made with due consideration of their long-term impact rather than focusing solely on immediate outcomes, he added.
He said the acceptability of the committee's decisions would depend on the wisdom, experience, and foresight of its members.
Principal Information Officer Syed Abdal Ahmed chaired the meeting, which was attended by Secretary (Routine Duty) of the Ministry of Information and Broadcasting Md Shah Alam, senior ministry officials, and members of the Press Accreditation Committee.
